name: Kushim al-Eanna
title: A Brief Life of Kushim al-Eanna
story: Kushim al-Eanna was born in 3245 BCE in the Eanna district of Uruk, within sight of the rising temple platforms dedicated to Inanna. His father brewed beer for temple laborers; his mother wove wool textiles issued as rations to workers and craftsmen.

At age 8 he entered the tablet house. There he learned to knead river clay to the right firmness, cut reed styluses, and master the complex numerical systems used for grain, animals, and labor allocations. By 3231 BCE, at fourteen, he was copying commodity lists and ration tallies for senior scribes.

In 3218 BCE, at age 27, he became overseer of barley receipts for the Eanna temple complex. From that year forward, tablets bearing the simple signature "Kushim" certified vast movements of goods through Uruk's economy.

During the height of his career, Kushim directed the accounting of the city's lifeblood. He tracked the inflow of harvest grain from surrounding fields, supervised the measurement and sealing of storage jars, authorized the distribution of daily beer rations to hundreds of laborers, recorded the tally of sheep and goats brought for temple sacrifice, and reconciled multi-year accounts spanning dozens of lunar months. His seal, rolled across wet clay, signified that the count was correct and the transaction complete.
In 3196 BCE he married Ama-šag, a skilled weaver attached to the temple workshops. They had three children, two of whom survived into adulthood.
In 3188 BCE, when canal flooding damaged granaries near the eastern embankment, Kushim coordinated emergency redistribution to sustain rebuilding crews and prevent unrest.

He died in 3182 BCE during a late-summer fever that swept through the lower districts of Uruk. He was buried beyond the city wall in a modest brick-lined grave. His cylinder seal was placed beside him.
The walls of Uruk eroded. The canals shifted. The temples fell and rose again. But clay remembers. And on baked tablets, pressed five thousand years ago, the name Kushim endures.
